channel.title: Ekky Defa Rizkyan
channel.description: Recent content on Ekky Defa Rizkyan

item.title: Redefining Quipper’s Performance Testing Platform
item.description:
How we cut execution time from 2 hours → 15 minutes and saved $60K+/year 🚀

Performance testing at scale is rarely glamorous. It’s slow, unpredictable, and often under-invested, until something breaks. When I joined Quipper, our performance-testing workflow was functional but far from efficient. Tests took nearly 2 hours to run, required significant engineering effort, and consumed more infrastructure than we were comfortable with. And as our products grew, the process was increasingly hard to justify.

The problem

Before the redesign, our performance-testing workflow was slow, expensive, and almost entirely manual. Running a single test felt like assembling a lab experiment from scratch, every time.

Here’s how it worked:

1. Everything was manual

Nothing about the workflow was automated. Every test run required a human QA engineer to perform dozens of manual steps just to get things going.

2. Manual file preparation + upload

To start a test, the QA team had to manually prepare and upload all JMeter artifacts, such as:

  • .jmx test plans
  • .csv dataset files
  • .test configuration assets

These files were uploaded to S3, only to be manually pulled down again later on the servers where the test would run. This workflow was repetitive and time-consuming.

3. Overreliance on AWS WorkSpaces (and its high cost)

The entire test environment ran on AWS WorkSpaces, which is essentially a fully managed virtual desktop (VDI), great for office workers, not so great for distributed load testing.

For each performance test:

  • QA engineers had to spin up 25 WorkSpace nodes
  • Each had to be started manually
  • And shut down manually

Beyond the friction, WorkSpaces was overpriced for this use case.

📊 Cost Estimation:

A Windows WorkSpace (PowerPro bundle) is:
• $7.25 monthly base fee per instance
• $1.53/hour usage
• $1.53/hr × 25 instances × 2 hours/test = $76.50/run

If QA runs the test once per day:
• $76.50 × 30 days = $2,295/month in usage fees
• Plus $7.25 × 25 instances = $181.25 in monthly base fees
• Total: ~$2,476.25/month

If tests run twice per day:
• $153 × 30 days = $4,590/month in usage fees
• Plus $181.25 in monthly base fees
• Total: ~$4,771.25/month

That's only the compute cost — storage, data transfer, and base fees add more.

Realistic total costs (including overhead):
• ~$6,000–$8,000/month
• Up to ~$72,000–$96,000/year

And that's only for performance testing.

Furthermore:

  • WorkSpaces billing rounds hourly
    • If you run 61 minutes, you pay 2 hours
  • Billing confusion + manual shutdowns often led to over-billing
  • QA was limited to working within time windows to keep cost down

We were spending cloud-desktop money to run distributed load testing and paying for the privilege of doing it manually.

4. Manual distribution of test artifacts

Once WorkSpaces were up, the QA engineer still had to:

  • Connect to the first node using AWS Console
  • Download the test assets from S3
  • Run 16 separate Python files to distribute the test assets to each node

If anything failed, the QA had to restart the distribution manually. There was no retry automation, no integrity checking, and no self-healing.

5. Manual execution of JMeter

After distribution, the QA would initiate JMeter from the first instance. This instance acted as the controller, coordinating 24 secondary nodes. Any misconfiguration, file mismatch, missing dependency, wrong path, etc., requires starting over.

6. Manual result upload

Once the test finished:

  • The QA exported results
  • Manually uploaded them back to S3
  • They have to download it manually to their local machine

Only after that could anyone analyze the output or generate reports.

7. Manual shutdown

Finally, and critically! All 25 WorkSpaces had to be shut down manually! If someone forgot, WorkSpaces kept charging by the hour. And because of hourly billing rounding, even short mistakes were costly.

All of those steps took about 2 hours to complete!

This meant:

  • Long iteration cycles
  • Low test frequency
  • Higher risk of missed regressions
  • High operational burden

❗ 📉 Performance testing wasn’t a tool, it was a tax. It’s wasting available resources: time, money, and man-hours.

The Approach

Once we identified that the real bottleneck wasn’t the testing tool, but the process, the goal became simple:

Make performance testing fast, automated, flexible, and cost-efficient

Instead of patching the old process, I decided to rebuild it from the ground up using Infrastructure as Code, automation, and lightweight orchestration.

1. Infrastructure Redesign with Terraform + GitHub Actions

The first step was to replace AWS WorkSpaces entirely. They were slow, expensive, and ill-suited for distributed automation.

I migrated the testing infrastructure to AWS EC2, provisioning all resources through Terraform. This gave us a clean, declarative setup where every environment could be built, destroyed, or modified automatically and repeatably.

To make the process fully automated and auditable:

  • Terraform runs are executed directly through GitHub Actions
  • All infrastructure changes are committed and tracked in our IaC repository
  • Any updates to configuration automatically trigger deployments

Every test environment is now ephemeral: created on demand, and destroyed after use.

Each EC2 instance is pre-configured automatically via user-data scripts, which install:

  • JMeter
  • Dependencies (Python, monitoring agents, etc.)
  • Environment-specific configurations

This means the QA engineer doesn’t need to install or configure anything manually, the infrastructure configures itself.

2. Simplifying Configuration for QA Engineers

While Terraform gave us automation, it wasn’t friendly to non-engineers. So I designed the setup around a single configuration file local.tf. which exposes all the tunable parameters the QA team might need:

 # General Configuration
 region = "us-east-1"
 jmeter_version = "5.6.3"
 bucket_name = "performance-test-bucket" # Only change this if required, otherwise let it be
 instance_tag = "performance_test" # This must be have same value as in python distributor, autimatically set worker and master tag
 ami_id = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux_2023.id # Amazon Linux 2023 AMI (dynamically fetched)
 # Change ami_id if you need to change instances OS, but make sure to update the user_data on instance.tf

 # Worker Configuration
 instance_count = 2
 worker_instance_type = "c6g.large" # You can check instance types on https://instances.vantage.sh/

 # Master Configuration
 enable_master = true # Set this to false if you going to use your local machine as master
 master_instance_type = "c6g.large" # You can check instance types on https://instances.vantage.sh/

All critical parameters, such as the number of nodes, instance type, tags, JMeter version can be adjusted from one place. QA engineers don’t have to touch multiple .tf files or understand Terraform internals.

This abstraction turned infrastructure setup into something simple, safe, and self-serve.

No SRE intervention needed.

3. Introducing PyDist: the Python Controller

Once the infrastructure was automated, I built a Python-based orchestration tool we call PyDist (Python Distributor) as the control layer that manages the entire testing lifecycle. PyDist connects everything into a single, reliable workflow.

Here’s what it does:

1. Configuration Validation

Utilizes Boto3 to verify that:

  • All EC2 nodes (master + workers) are running and reachable
  • S3 buckets are accessible
  • Connections between master and worker nodes are healthy

2. File Distribution

Automatically uploads all .jmx files, .csv variable files, and configurations to the appropriate nodes. No more manual S3 uploads or remote copy scripts.

3. Service Control

Starts and stops the JMeter service on each EC2 instance, ensuring consistent test environments every time.

4. Test Execution

Runs CLI-based JMeter tests from the master node, coordinating all worker nodes in distributed mode. PyDist also monitors progress and collects test logs in real-time.

5. Result Management

Once tests complete:

  • Results are automatically uploaded to S3
  • PyDist downloads the results to the local machine for analysis
  • It can also re-download past test results if needed for reporting or regression comparison

In essence, PyDist replaced dozens of manual steps with a single command and brought order, visibility, and repeatability to the whole process.

4. Cost Optimization

By transitioning from GUI to CLI mode and optimizing our infrastructure, we achieved remarkable performance improvements:

  • Test Execution Time:

    • Light tests: 30-40 minutes → 3-5 minutes (8-13x faster)
    • Heavy tests: 45-60 minutes → 5-10 minutes (6-12x faster)

  • Resource Utilization:

    • Reduced from 25 Windows instances to just 4 Linux instances
    • Each node handles significantly more load due to CLI efficiency
    • ARM-based instances provide better price/performance ratio

Cost-Effective Infrastructure

Our new architecture delivers substantial cost savings:

Resource Old (WorkSpaces) New (EC2) Savings
Node Type Windows PowerPro (2vCPU, 8GB) c6g.large (2vCPU, 4GB) -
Node Count 25 4 (1 Master + 3 Workers) 84% reduction
Monthly Cost (On-Demand) ~$6,000-$8,000 ~$220 96-97% savings
Annual Cost ~$72,000-$96,000 ~$2,640 96-97% savings

Smart Automation with GitHub Actions

To maximize cost efficiency, we implemented two key workflows:

  1. Auto-Shutdown (Nightly)

    • Automatically powers down all test instances at midnight
    • Prevents unnecessary charges for idle resources
    • Runs as a scheduled GitHub Action

  2. On-Demand Cluster Startup

    • Single-click deployment via GitHub Actions
    • No AWS console access or Terraform knowledge required
    • Consistent, repeatable environment setup

The Impact of Auto-Shutdown

While the base EC2 costs are already significantly lower, auto-shutdown provides additional savings:

  • Typical Usage Scenario:
    • 8 hours/day, 20 days/month = 160 hours of usage
    • Without auto-shutdown: $220/month (24/7 uptime)
    • With auto-shutdown: ~$48/month (160 hours)
    • Additional savings: ~78% off already reduced costs

This combination of performance optimization, right-sized infrastructure, and smart automation ensures we’re not just saving money, but also getting faster, more reliable test results.

5. The Outcome

By combining Terraform, GitHub Actions, and PyDist, we turned a clunky, manual workflow into a streamlined, automated, and maintainable system.

  • No more manual uploads or remote logins
  • Fully documented and version-controlled infrastructure
  • Self-service configuration for QA
  • Automated start/stop + cost control
  • Reliable, repeatable, fast test runs

And the result?

  • Test cycle time reduced from 2 hours → 15 minutes 🚀
  • ~$60,000+/year in infrastructure savings 💸
  • Plus unmeasured (but huge) savings in time, manual effort, and frustration 😃

Lesson Learned

Rebuilding the performance-testing platform taught me that real reliability isn’t about tools, it’s about systems. The old workflow didn’t fail because JMeter was bad; it failed because the process around it wasn’t built to scale.

Here’s what stood out the most:

1. Fix the process, not just the tool

Automation works best when it removes human dependency. By redesigning the workflow we turned a manual process into a repeatable system.

2. IaC is communication, not just code

Terraform made every change visible, reviewable, and reversible. It replaced tribal knowledge with a shared, documented truth.

3. Empower, don’t gatekeep

By simplifying configuration for QA, we gave ownership back to the team. They could run, tweak, and iterate without waiting on SREs.

4. Build cost efficiency into design

We stopped relying on people to remember to shut things down. With automation, cost-saving became an inherent behavior, not an afterthought.

5. Make testing a habit

When tests are fast and simple, people run them often. That’s how reliability becomes part of the culture, not a special event.

Final Take

What started as a slow, frustrating process became an elegant system that just works. Tests that once took hours now finish in minutes, infrastructure spins up and down on demand, and engineers can focus on insight instead of setup.

But the biggest win isn’t the speed or the savings: it’s the shift in mindset. Performance testing stopped being a chore and became a shared discipline.

Thank you for taking the time to read about this journey. I hope you found some useful insights or inspiration in these pages. If you’re working on similar challenges, I’d love to hear about your experiences.

After all, the best solutions often come from sharing knowledge and learning from each other.

item.title: About
item.description:
Hi, I’m Ekky, currently a Senior Site Reliability Engineer.

I’m a lifelong learner, a systems thinker, and a builder at heart.

My journey in tech has always been driven by curiosity. I don’t just work with systems, I obsess over how they behave, how they break, and how to make them better, faster, and more resilient. Whether it’s infrastructure, automation, security, observability, or developer tooling, I find joy in diving deep into complexity and emerging with clarity, structure, and solutions that make a real impact.

What defines me most is my love of learning. I’m the kind of person who reads RFCs for fun, browses Medium and Wikipedia late at night, and gets excited by a good problem, technical or philosophical. I thrive when I’m learning something new or helping someone else solve a thorny problem. I believe that every system, whether technical, organizational, or human, can be understood, improved, and scaled with the right mindset and approach.

I call myself a philomath, someone who finds joy in learning anything. Outside of tech, I’m deeply interested in philosophy, history, psychology, nature, and human systems. I believe that well-rounded engineers aren’t just great coders or architects, they’re communicators, thinkers, and collaborators who can bridge disciplines and work with diverse people toward common goals.

I’m also passionate about:

  • Building tools that empower others and remove friction from development workflows
  • Making infrastructure and operations more accessible, scalable, and reliable through code
  • Sharing knowledge and mentoring others in the community or within teams
  • Designing systems that don’t just “work,” but evolve well under pressure and change
  • Balancing technical rigour with empathy for real-world use cases and developer experience

If there’s one thing I aim for in my career, it’s to be the kind of engineer that teammates can rely on, not just to write solid code or manage infrastructure, but to ask the right questions, drive clarity, and leave systems (and teams) better than I found them.

Outside of work, you’ll find me exploring new topics, watching deep-dive YouTube content, hanging out with friends,working on DIY side projects, or just going out and explore the world.

Contact

  • Email: defa.ekky1073@gmail.com
  • LinkedIn: https://linkedin.com/in/defaekky1994
  • GitHub: https://github.com/defa-ekky1073

item.title: Professional Experience
item.description:
Quipper | Senior Site Reliability Engineer

Remote, 2022 — Present

As the Senior SRE, I own the reliability and developer-experience charter across multi-cloud infrastructure, pushing automation, observability, and CI/CD maturity so teams can ship faster with confidence.

  • Migrated CI/CD from CircleCI to GitHub Actions with self-hosted runners, saving ~$6K/month and enabling reusable workflows.
  • Cut end-to-end tests from 2 hours to 15 minutes (≈$60K/year savings) and lifted developer productivity by 83% through pipeline instrumentation and deployment insights.
  • Hardened public endpoints via AWS CloudFront + WAF and delivered an AI-powered documentation copilot that reduces SRE support load.

Tech Stacks: AWS, GCP, Azure, Terraform, Ansible, Docker, Kubernetes, Python, GitHub, GitHub Action, CircleCI, Jenkins, ArgoCD, Datadog, Sentry, OpenSearch, MongoDB, PostgreSQL

eFishery | DevOps Engineer

Remote, 2021 — 2022

I partnered with product and platform teams to scale Nomad-based infrastructure and build Go-powered tooling that tightened feedback loops while keeping 700+ containers running.

  • Maintained 30+ Nomad nodes, enhanced custom CI tooling, and shipped developer productivity utilities in Go.
  • Migrated sensitive workflows to HashiCorp Vault and integrated secrets into bespoke CI/CD paths.
  • Designed centralized ElasticStack logging for 70+ apps with 4B daily log ingests, accelerating troubleshooting and security detection.

Tech Stacks: Docker, AWS, GCP, GO, Nomad, Consul, Hashicorp Vault, Gitlab, Gitlab CI, Jenkins, ELK Stacks, Prometheus, Grafana, Odoo, Printunl Zero, MongoDB

Halofina | System Engineer

Bandung - Indonesia, 2019 — 2021

I steered infrastructure availability and security for a fast-growing fintech stack, blending proactive monitoring with automation to keep services resilient.

  • Tuned AWS/GCP environments end to end: OS hardening, redundancy, failover, and observability.
  • Authored automation scripts, tracked performance bottlenecks, and partnered with devs to deploy fixes quickly.
  • Oversaw backups, disaster-recovery drills, and monitoring pipelines to sustain compliance-grade uptime.

Tech Stacks: Docker, AWS, GCP, Kubernetes, Hashicorp Vault, BitBucket, BitBucket Pipeline, ELK Stacks, Galera Database Cluster, OpenVPN, NGINX

Halofina | Backend Engineer

Bandung - Indonesia, 2019

Earlier, I focused on shipping secure APIs and the platform-specific libraries that enabled faster fintech module delivery, while leading a PHP-to-Java migration.

  • Built and documented REST services with supporting libraries to streamline new feature work.
  • Drove the migration from PHP to Java Vert.x for better performance and maintainability, backed by unit tests.

Tech Stacks: PHP, Java, Flight Micro-Framework, Vert.x, Postman, Cypress, Kafka, MySQL

SirusPlus | Backend Engineer

Bandung - Indonesia, 2018 — 2019

At SirusPlus I delivered modular Node.js/TypeScript APIs for healthcare workflows, ensuring they could evolve from monolith to microservices when needed.

  • Crafted REST and GraphQL endpoints with unit/E2E coverage, ready for future service decomposition.
  • Coordinated cross-functional deliverables (scope, budget, timelines) for hospital registration features.

Tech Stacks: NodeJs, Express, GraphQL, Postman, RabbitMQ, Gitlab, MySQL

Lussa Teknologi Global | Backend Engineer

Bandung - Indonesia, 2017 - 2018

My work at Lussa spanned healthcare, automation, and blockchain prototypes, supporting founders with production-ready APIs built for security and performance.

  • Produced and maintained secure APIs powering web/mobile products, scaling from concept to launch.
  • Collaborated with product teams to translate experiments into hardened services with proper safeguards.

Tech Stacks: NodeJs, Express, GraphQL, Postman, MySQL

Skills Overview
Cloud Platforms AWS · GCP · Azure
Programming Languages & Runtimes Go · Python · Java · JavaScript/TypeScript · PHP · Bash
Backend & API Frameworks Node.js · Express · Vert.x · GraphQL · Flight Micro-Framework
Databases & Messaging PostgreSQL · MySQL · MongoDB · Galera Cluster · RabbitMQ · Kafka
Infrastructure, IaC & Secrets Terraform · Ansible · Chef · HashiCorp Vault · HashiCorp Consul
Containerization & Orchestration Docker · Kubernetes · Nomad
CI/CD & Platform Tooling GitHub · GitHub Actions · GitLab · GitLab CI/CD · Bitbucket · Bitbucket Pipelines · CircleCI · Jenkins · Argo CD
Observability & Monitoring Elastic Stack · OpenSearch · Prometheus · Grafana · Datadog · Sentry · CloudWatch
Networking, Edge & Access NGINX · OpenVPN · Printunl Zero
Testing Postman · Cypress

 

📄 Download My CV

Last updated: May 2026

For the most up-to-date information, please visit my LinkedIn profile.
